claimed_contribution: The paper identifies food-induced serotonin variation as a driver of consumer impulsivity, beyond the usual focus on store context and trait differences.
It provides converging evidence from a naturalistic Thanksgiving-Black Friday setting and a controlled randomized lab study using a tryptophan-containing beverage.
It shows that serotonergic effects are stronger for high dispositional impulsives and appear in both impulsive choice and response inhibition measures.
context: Study 1 is set on Thanksgiving evening, when many participants consume turkey and carbohydrate-rich holiday meals, and links this to Black Friday discount purchase intentions. Study 2 is a laboratory setting with fasting participants who consume standardized beverages before completing decision tasks.
design_and_data: Study 1 used an online survey on Thanksgiving evening with 170 participants, comparing those who reported a traditional turkey dinner (n = 113) versus other dinners (n = 57). Participants rated willingness to buy a deeply discounted laptop, completed a dispositional impulsivity scale and PANAS, and reported next-day shopping intention; analyses tested condition effects and condition x impulsivity interactions. Study 2 recruited 54 participants (one excluded for refusing the drink), randomly assigned to a tryptophan-rich chocolate milk-based protein shake (650 mg tryptophan) or a similar control shake, then tested them 2.5 hours later after fasting. Outcomes were go/no-go performance (commission and omission errors, response time, earnings) and a layaway-versus-credit-card financing preference task.
main_findings: In Study 1, participants who consumed the tryptophan-rich Thanksgiving meal were less willing to buy the discounted laptop than control participants (M = 3.96 vs. 4.63), and mood did not differ across groups on PANAS.

The Study 1 effect was moderated by dispositional impulsivity: high-impulsives showed a large reduction in willingness to buy after tryptophan-rich consumption (M = 3.79 vs. 5.32), whereas low-impulsives showed no significant difference.

In Study 2, the tryptophan group made fewer go/no-go commission errors (6.66% vs. 18.07%), earned more money, had longer response times on bad-number trials, showed no omission-error differences, and preferred layaway more strongly (M = 7.52 vs. 6.25).
one_line_summary: Across a Thanksgiving field setting and a randomized lab experiment, the paper shows that consuming tryptophan-rich food or drink is linked to lower impulsive consumer choice and better inhibition of impulsive responses.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior serotonin research from psychopathology and amino-acid manipulation paradigms to everyday consumer decisions by showing that ordinary tryptophan-rich meals and drinks can reduce impulsive product and financing choices.
research_gap: Prior consumer research mainly explains impulsive choice through contextual triggers and personality traits, while serotonin research has focused largely on clinical or nonconsumer behaviors. The paper addresses whether food-based serotonin enhancement can shape everyday consumer impulsive choice and responding.
research_question: Does consuming tryptophan-rich food that enhances serotonin reduce consumers' impulsive choice and impulsive responding? Is this effect stronger for consumers high in dispositional impulsivity than for those low in dispositional impulsivity?
